title: A Visible-Frequency Excitonic Reststrahlen Band in (PEA)$_2$PbI$_4$ Slabs
authors: Michael Pfeufer, Patrick Grenzer, Friedrich Schöppler, Tobias Hertel
abstract: Layered halide perovskites host exceptionally strong excitons, whose optical signatures are usually interpreted as absorptive resonances on a smooth dielectric background. Strong excitons, however, can also reshape the dielectric response itself and drive the real permittivity negative, opening a reflective band: the visible, excitonic analogue of an infrared Reststrahlen band. Whether bare (PEA)$_2$PbI$_4$ slabs reach this regime has remained unclear. Here we show that low-temperature transmission of (PEA)$_2$PbI$_4$ slabs, driven by the intralayer-exciton manifold, evolves with increasing thickness from an excitonic dip into a broad near-zero-transmission interval with compressed Fabry-Pérot-like fringes. Transfer-matrix analysis with an effective Lorentz-oscillator dielectric response reproduces this crossover, reconstructs a finite negative-Re($\varepsilon$) window, and implies near-ultrastrong exciton-photon coupling. Calculated field maps show suppressed in-plane field penetration within this interval and a driven longitudinal response near the high-energy ($\varepsilon=0$) edge. These results identify (PEA)$_2$PbI$_4$ slabs as a cavity-free visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen material.
summary: This paper investigates the optical response of $(	ext{PEA})_2	ext{PbI}_4$ perovskite slabs, demonstrating a thickness-dependent transition from simple excitonic absorption to a visible-frequency stop band. By modeling the dielectric response with a Lorentz oscillator, the authors confirm the existence of a negative real permittivity window, classifying the material as a natural excitonic Reststrahlen medium.
main_problem: The study aims to determine if the intrinsic exciton oscillator strength in layered perovskites is sufficient to drive the real permittivity ($	ext{Re}(\epsilon)$) negative, thereby creating a visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen-like stop band.
main_result: The authors show that $(	ext{PEA})_2	ext{PbI}_4$ slabs exhibit a thickness-dependent crossover from an excitonic dip to a broad near-zero-transmission interval, confirming its status as a cavity-free visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen material.
method: The research combines low-temperature transmission spectroscopy of varying thickness slabs with transfer-matrix analysis using an effective Lorentz-oscillator dielectric response model.
model_or_system: The physical system is the layered halide perovskite $(	ext{PEA})_2	ext{PbI}_4$. The optical response is modeled using a Lorentz-oscillator form for the dielectric function, $\epsilon(E)$, which is then propagated through the slab thickness using transfer-matrix methods.
key_observables: Transmittance spectra, the development of a finite negative-$	ext{Re}(\epsilon)$ window, suppressed in-plane field penetration ($|E_\parallel|^2$), and enhanced surface-normal field ($|E_\perp|^2$).
important_parameters: Low temperature (10 K), slab thickness ($d$), and the Rabi splitting ($\hbar\Omega_R \approx 0.5 	ext{ eV}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the intralayer exciton manifold is the dominant oscillator driving the optical response, and the negative permittivity window is responsible for the stop-band behavior.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the thickness-dependent crossover from an excitonic dip to a stop-band, the reconstructed negative-$	ext{Re}(\epsilon)$ window, and calculated field maps showing field suppression/enhancement within this band.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first observing the thickness-dependent spectral evolution (experiment), then modeling this crossover using a Lorentz-oscillator dielectric response (theory), and finally interpreting the resulting negative permittivity as evidence for a visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen band.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the strong light-matter coupling regime in solid-state materials, providing experimental and theoretical evidence for an excitonic stop band analogous to the infrared Reststrahlen effect, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Excitonic energy scales and slab-transmission phenomenology of PEPI. (a) Inverted normalized trans- mittance spectrum of a 𝑑= 210 nm slab at 10 K, showing a stop-band-like response near the intralayer exciton. (b) Room- and low-temperature PL spectra of a ∼200 nm slab, resolving at 8 K a trion-like feature near 2.30 eV and the intralayer-exciton manifold near 2.34 eV. Inset: expanded PL of a comparable flake showing a weak doublet near 2.38 eV, assigned in PL to interlayer excitons.11 (c) Temperature-dependent normalized transmittance of a 𝑑= 388 nm slab from 10 to 300 K. Figure 2: Transfer-matrix modeling of the thickness- dependent transmittance. (a) Model workflow combining an effective Lorentz-oscillator dielectric response, transfer-matrix propagation through the air/PEPI/sapphire stack, and parameter extraction from measured spectra. (b) Normalized transmittance spectra and fits at 𝑇= 10 K for representative slab thicknesses of 24 nm, 388 nm, and 18.0 𝜇m.

Figure 3: (a) Bulk-like polariton dispersion derived from the reconstructed dielectric response of a representative 520 nm slab at 10 K, together with the vacuum light cone (solid blue), the corresponding 45◦light line (dashed blue), the uncoupled bulk-photon dispersion (green dashed), and the calculated finite-slab exciton–polariton branches (gray). The resulting Rabi splitting between upper and lower branches is indicated by ℏΩ𝑅. (b) Mode structure calculated for the fitted 𝑑= 520 nm slab at 10 K, shown together with the corresponding experimental transmittance spectrum. Figure 4: Calculated 𝑝-polarized field distributions, 𝑝-polarized slab-mode structure and dielectric response for a 196 nm PEPI slab on a substrate. Panels (a,b) show false color plots of the field intensities parallel and perpendicular to the slab surface, |𝐸∥|2 and |𝐸⊥|2, as functions of photon energy and distance from the air/slab interface.
